definition: The ratio of safety stock to average demand, representing the degree of inventory-based buffering against supply chain variability.

Binary indicator of whether a supply chain disruption event occurred in a given period. Disruptions include supplier failures, logistics interruptions, demand shocks, natural disasters, geopolitical events, and cyber attacks.
formal_definition: D ∈ {0, 1} where D=1 iff one or more supply chain disruption events occurred in observation period t.
operational_definition: Coded from SEC 8-K filings, news event databases (GDELT), or proprietary supply chain monitoring platforms.

definition: Composite measure of disruption impact capturing magnitude of operational, financial, and reputational consequences. Integrates revenue loss, recovery time, and downstream propagation extent.
formal_definition: DSI = f(revenue_impact, recovery_time, cascade_depth, customer_impact) normalized to [0, 100].
operational_definition: Weighted composite of percentage revenue decline, days to recovery, number of downstream firms affected, and customer service level degradation.

definition: Duration required for a supply chain node to restore full operational capacity following a disruption. Central metric in the Simchi-Levi Risk Exposure Index framework.
formal_definition: TTR = t_recovery - t_disruption, measured in days/weeks.
operational_definition: Time from disruption onset to return to pre-disruption production/service levels, measured via operational KPIs.

definition: Maximum duration a downstream node can continue operations after an upstream disruption, given current inventory buffers and alternative sourcing capacity.
formal_definition: TTS = max{t : demand_t can be met from inventory + alternative sources without the disrupted node}.
operational_definition: Computed from safety stock levels, demand rate, and available substitute supplier capacity.

definition: Degree to which a focal firm has access to timely and accurate information about supply chain partners' operations, inventories, and risk exposures across multiple tiers.
operational_definition: Measured via survey instruments (1-5 Likert scales) or as percentage of Tier 1/2/3 suppliers with real-time data sharing agreements.

definition: Composite measure of a supply chain's capacity to anticipate, prepare for, respond to, and recover from disruptions while maintaining continuous operations.
formal_definition: R = f(readiness, responsiveness, recovery_capability, adaptive_capacity).
operational_definition: Multi-criteria composite integrating supplier diversification, inventory buffers, visibility, flexibility, and recovery track record.

definition: Number or proportion of supply chain nodes rendered non-functional following an initial disruption, measuring the extent of downstream propagation through the network.
formal_definition: C = |{nodes failed}| / |{total nodes}| after removal/failure of seed node(s).
operational_definition: Measured via percolation analysis or agent-based simulation on supply network graphs.

definition: Quantitative measure comparing Time-To-Recover to Time-To-Survive for each supply chain node. Nodes where TTR > TTS represent critical vulnerabilities. Developed by Simchi-Levi et al. and implemented at Ford Motor Company.
formal_definition: REI_node = max(0, TTR - TTS). Aggregate REI = Σ REI_node × impact_weight.
operational_definition: Computed from supplier-level TTR estimates and inventory-based TTS calculations.

definition: Degree to which supply chain nodes are spatially concentrated in a limited number of geographic regions, increasing vulnerability to localized disruptions (natural disasters, geopolitical events, pandemics).
formal_definition: GCR = HHI of supplier locations across regions = Σ s_i², where s_i is share of suppliers in region i.
operational_definition: Herfindahl-Hirschman Index computed over supplier facility locations grouped by country or sub-national region.

definition: Measure of a node's importance in a supply network based on the fraction of shortest paths between all other node pairs that pass through it. High betweenness indicates a critical chokepoint whose failure would disrupt many supply relationships.
formal_definition: BC(v) = Σ_{s≠v≠t} σ_st(v) / σ_st, where σ_st is total shortest paths from s to t and σ_st(v) are those passing through v.
operational_definition: Computed from supply chain network graph using firm-level buyer-supplier relationship data.

definition: Global measure of spatial autocorrelation in trade flow or supply chain activity data. Positive values indicate geographic clustering of similar trade intensities; negative values indicate spatial dispersion.
formal_definition: I = (N/W) × (Σ_i Σ_j w_ij(x_i - x̄)(x_j - x̄)) / (Σ_i(x_i - x̄)²), where w_ij are spatial weights.
operational_definition: Computed using queen/rook contiguity or k-nearest neighbor spatial weights on country-level or port-level trade volume data.

definition: Composite indicator of a supplier's financial stability and creditworthiness, predicting likelihood of operational failure or inability to fulfill orders.
formal_definition: SFH = f(Altman Z-score, current_ratio, debt_equity, operating_margin, revenue_trend).
operational_definition: Weighted composite of publicly available financial ratios from annual reports, credit agency scores (D&B, S&P), or ML-predicted default probability.

definition: Coefficient of variation of actual delivery lead times relative to contracted/expected lead times. Higher variability indicates unreliable supply and amplifies bullwhip effects.
formal_definition: LTV = σ(lead_time) / μ(lead_time).
operational_definition: Standard deviation of delivery lead times divided by mean, computed from purchase order and goods receipt timestamps.

definition: Value or quantity of bilateral trade between countries or firms, serving as the primary measure of supply chain connectivity and economic interdependence.
formal_definition: T_ij = total value of exports from i to j in period t (USD, current or constant).
operational_definition: Measured from UN Comtrade, national customs data, or firm-level transaction records.

definition: Measure of geopolitical instability and policy uncertainty affecting supply chain operations, including sanctions, trade wars, armed conflicts, and regime changes.
formal_definition: GPR_t = f(conflict_events, sanction_intensity, political_instability, trade_policy_uncertainty) in period t.
operational_definition: Caldara & Iacoviello GPR Index (text-based from newspaper articles), or composite of ACLED conflict data, sanctions databases, and WGI political stability scores.

definition: Measure of supply chain vulnerability to climate change impacts including extreme weather events, sea level rise, water scarcity, and temperature extremes affecting production and logistics.
operational_definition: Composite of ND-GAIN Country Index, facility-level flood/hurricane/heat risk scores, and historical climate event frequency at supplier locations.

definition: Phenomenon where demand variability amplifies as it propagates upstream through supply chain echelons. Small fluctuations in consumer demand produce increasingly large swings in orders placed by upstream suppliers.
formal_definition: BWE = Var(orders_upstream) / Var(demand_downstream) > 1.
operational_definition: Ratio of order variance at upstream tier to demand variance at downstream tier, measured across 2+ supply chain echelons.

definition: Disruption propagation through supply chain networks where a localized failure cascades downstream, affecting multiple tiers and functions. Distinguished from bullwhip by being disruption-driven rather than information-driven.
formal_definition: Disruption at node v propagates to downstream nodes D(v) with probability p(v,u) for each edge (v,u).
operational_definition: Measured via simulation or empirical tracking of disruption impact across supply chain tiers.

definition: Geographic spread of supply chain disruptions where problems in one location increase the probability of disruptions in nearby locations through shared infrastructure, common suppliers, or correlated hazards.
operational_definition: Detected via LISA cluster maps showing statistically significant spatial clustering of disruption events, or spatial lag model coefficients.

definition: Number and distribution of alternative suppliers available for critical inputs, measuring redundancy in the supply base as a buffer against single-source failures.
formal_definition: SD = 1 - HHI(supplier_shares) for each critical input category.
operational_definition: Inverse HHI of procurement spend across suppliers per commodity category.

definition: Degree of reliance on geographically concentrated or scarce mineral inputs essential for production. High dependency on minerals with concentrated extraction/refining (e.g., cobalt, rare earths, lithium) creates structural vulnerability.
operational_definition: Import dependency ratio × HHI of source countries for each critical mineral input, using USGS/IEA production data.

definition: NY Federal Reserve composite index measuring global supply chain conditions using transportation costs and manufacturing indicators. Positive values indicate above-normal pressure; negative values indicate below-normal conditions.
formal_definition: GSCPI_t = principal component extracted from Baltic Dry Index, Harpex Index, airfreight costs, PMI delivery times, PMI backlogs, PMI purchased stocks across regions.
operational_definition: Monthly index published by NY Fed, standardized with mean 0 and SD 1 relative to 1998-2019 baseline.

definition: Multi-criteria composite measuring overall supply chain exposure to disruption, integrating geographic concentration, supplier financial health, network structure, demand volatility, and external threat environment.
operational_definition: MCDA-constructed index using AHP/TOPSIS weighting of sub-indicators, normalized and aggregated per OECD composite indicator guidelines.

definition: Vulnerability of digital supply chain infrastructure to cyber attacks including ransomware, data breaches, IoT compromise, and third-party software vulnerabilities.
operational_definition: Composite of vendor cybersecurity rating scores (BitSight, SecurityScorecard), count of third-party software dependencies, and incident history.

definition: Measure of shipping bottleneck severity at port facilities, reflecting vessel queue times, berth utilization, and container dwell times that constrain supply chain throughput.
operational_definition: Composite of average vessel waiting days, container dwell time, and berth utilization rate from AIS data and port authority reports.

definition: Number of sequential supplier tiers between raw material extraction and final product delivery. Greater depth increases complexity, reduces visibility, and amplifies disruption propagation.
formal_definition: TD = max shortest path length from any raw material source to focal firm in the supply network graph.
operational_definition: Counted from supply chain mapping exercises or BOM (Bill of Materials) decomposition.

definition: Strategic reconfiguration of supply chain geography by relocating production or sourcing closer to end markets, reducing geographic concentration risk and lead time variability at potential cost of efficiency.
operational_definition: Measured as change in geographic distribution of supplier base (shift toward proximate countries) or as firm-level binary indicator of announced reshoring/nearshoring decisions.

definition: Degree of geographic and firm-level concentration in advanced semiconductor fabrication. TSMC produces 92% of chips below 7nm. Taiwan, South Korea, and Japan dominate global fab capacity, creating extreme single-point-of-failure risk.
formal_definition: SC_conc = HHI(fab_capacity_shares) across firms and countries for a given process node.
operational_definition: Share of global wafer fabrication capacity by firm and country at each process node (7nm, 5nm, 3nm), from IC Insights/TrendForce data.

definition: Degree to which production of active pharmaceutical ingredients is concentrated in a small number of countries. China and India produce approximately 80% of global APIs, creating structural vulnerability in pharmaceutical supply chains.
formal_definition: API_conc = HHI(api_production_shares) across countries.
operational_definition: Country-level share of global API production volume from FDA, EMA, and WHO pharmaceutical production databases.

definition: Interruption in the production, processing, distribution, or retail of food products causing shortages, price spikes, or food safety incidents. Includes agricultural production failures, logistics disruptions, and processing facility closures.
operational_definition: Binary indicator from FAO Food Price Crisis database, USDA food supply disruption reports, or news-based event detection.

definition: Projected demand for minerals critical to clean energy technologies (lithium, cobalt, nickel, rare earths, copper, graphite) driven by EV batteries, wind turbines, solar panels, and grid storage.
formal_definition: ETMD = Σ (technology_deployment_t × mineral_intensity_per_unit) across clean energy technologies.
operational_definition: IEA/IRENA projections of mineral demand under various energy transition scenarios (STEPS, APS, NZE).

definition: Assurance that defense system components are authentic, uncompromised, and sourced from trusted suppliers throughout the acquisition lifecycle. Encompasses anti-counterfeit measures, trusted foundry programs, and supply chain illumination.
operational_definition: Measured via NIST C-SCRM compliance level, DoD SCRM maturity assessment scores, or counterfeit part incident rate.

definition: Simultaneous supply-side disruption (facility closures, labor shortages, logistics breakdowns) and demand-side shock (panic buying, demand shifts, sector reallocation) caused by pandemic events. Creates unprecedented bullwhip amplification.
operational_definition: Measured as deviation from baseline demand and supply indices during WHO-declared pandemic periods.

definition: Uncertainty about future trade policy (tariffs, quotas, sanctions, export controls) that increases supply chain planning difficulty and risk. Measured through text-based indices from news coverage of trade policy.
formal_definition: TPU_t = count of trade-policy-related articles / total articles in major newspapers, scaled.
operational_definition: Baker-Bloom-Davis Economic Policy Uncertainty Index (trade policy component), or Caldara-Iacoviello GPR Index trade sub-component.

definition: Exposure of supply chain logistics to disruption at critical maritime chokepoints (Suez Canal, Strait of Malacca, Panama Canal, Strait of Hormuz, Bab el-Mandeb). Approximately 80% of global trade by volume transits through these narrow passages.
formal_definition: CV = Σ (trade_share_through_chokepoint_i × disruption_probability_i).
operational_definition: Share of a firm's or country's trade transiting each chokepoint, from AIS vessel tracking data and port-of-origin/destination records.

definition: Ease with which an alternative supplier can replace a disrupted one for a given input, considering technical specifications, qualification time, capacity, and cost. Low substitutability indicates lock-in and high disruption impact.
formal_definition: SS = f(1/qualification_time, alternative_capacity/demand, 1/switching_cost).
operational_definition: Composite of alternative supplier count, average qualification lead time (months), and cost premium for switching, from procurement databases.

definition: Supply chain carbon footprint vulnerability — regulatory risk from emissions reporting requirements (EU CBAM, SEC climate disclosure) and transition risk from carbon pricing affecting supplier costs.
operational_definition: Estimated Scope 3 upstream emissions (tCO2e) from supply chain activities, per GHG Protocol methodology, using industry-average emission factors or supplier-reported data.
